text: Welcome to what I thought was an absolutely fascinating case. I could not wait to do this. So look at the CT scan. My arrow is on the left SVC. So what patient would you like to have a left SVC? The answer is someone with a thymus because in a VATS thymectomy, the most difficult bit is getting it off the innominate vein. This person's got no innominate vein.

So I had to get out all the big guns. I had to get my best instruments out. And the best instruments are these. The Articential wristed instruments. I call it surgeon powered robotics. Why do I do that? Because I can do everything that I do with a robot with these wristed instruments. So a few little tips. This is the outside view. You really need to get your elbows at right angles and be nice and comfortable. They're quite long, although they're coming out with some shorter ones. And so I quite like to get the camera out of the way rather than in between them. So I put the camera below and then nice right angles. Beautiful. And look how easy these handles are. Up, down, left, right and then a grasper. So these are a dream.

Next few videos hopefully I will have a surgeon controlled camera holder as well. So it really will be surgeon powered robotics. I'll have a camera holder and wristed instruments. This is a little view with my arm a bit higher. The further away your ports are from the action then the smaller the movements just like a robot and then the closer the ports are to the action the bigger your movements will have to be. So here we go. I went on the left. I normally go on the right but I had to go on the left because of the left SVC.

The surgeon performs a series of intercostal blocks under direct vision using a green needle, targeting each intercostal space due to the patient's thin physique. The Maryland wristed instrument and Johan grasper are visible, both capable of applying bipolar energy.

The surgeon uses the Johan grasper to pull down tissue while applying diathermy with the Maryland instrument to remove fat from the back of the sternum. The procedure involves a complete thymectomy, including removal of the thymoma and addressing myasthenia gravis by excising the inferior and superior poles of the thymus.

The surgeon removes fat from the back of the sternum, aiming to create more space for the thymectomy. A glimpse of the left internal mammary artery is visible as the dissection progresses towards the diaphragm. The procedure is performed using a left VATS approach due to the presence of a left superior vena cava.

The surgeon uses wristed bipolar instruments to carefully dissect along the left superior vena cava (SVC), exposing the phrenic nerve while maintaining a safe margin. The instruments allow precise angling and minimal energy distribution, ensuring safe manipulation of the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection using Maryland instruments, ensuring a safe distance from the phrenic nerve before applying bipolar energy for cauterization. The instruments are used delicately to achieve precise tissue manipulation, with the other arm lifting tissue for better exposure.

The surgeon follows the left superior vena cava (SVC) while searching for the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) vein, angling up and over to join the typical track. The medial border of the left SVC is examined for a vestigial innominate vein, which is not visible in the operation.

The surgeon carefully follows the phrenic nerve downwards, inspecting the pre-thymic fat for n-one lymph nodes. Using Maryland instruments, the surgeon dissects the fat off the back of the sternum and begins to pull down the thymus. The dissection reveals the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) vein connecting to the left superior vena cava (SVC), visible to the right of the Maryland instrument.

The surgeon identifies the blood supply to the thymus from the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) and right internal mammary artery (RIMA), observing the veins and arteries entering the thymus. The surgeon uses a hook ligature instrument, capable of dividing vessels up to seven millimeters, to manage these vessels.

The surgeon transitions from using a ligature to a hook Ligashore to address a vessel within the tissue stalk. The tissue is carefully pulled away to reveal a vessel, likely one of the thymic vessels originating from the lemur stumps, before proceeding with division using the Ligashore.

The surgeon uses the hook diathermy part of the hook ligature to remove fat from the diaphragm, ensuring complete removal due to the patient's myasthenia. The surgeon alternates between the hook for speed and the ligature for hemostasis, pulling with the right hand while dividing fat with the left hand. The procedure progresses with the removal of fat from the back of the sternum and chest wall, preparing to locate the reamer.

The surgeon gains a view of the right hemithorax, using a combination of hooking and ligature techniques. The pericardium is pressed down as the thymus is carefully pulled away from the diaphragm at the junction with the pericardium.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to push thymic tissue away from the diaphragm, revealing the bottom of the pericardium. The diaphragm is visible to the left, and the thymus is being pulled up with the right hand, creating a clear separation from the diaphragm. The Maryland's bipolar function aids in further dissection along the pericardium, following a natural tissue plane.

The surgeon identifies a tissue plane in the non-tumor area and begins stripping it off the pericardium. The pericardium is liberally resected to ensure clear margins, preventing diffuse thymomas from reaching inked margins. The surgeon opens into the right pleura and tracks south towards the right diaphragm, using diathermy to dissect along the back of the sternum down to the diaphragm and across to the opposite side of the chest. The diaphragm is visible extending off to the side.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to divide and remove fatty tissue from the right hemidiaphragm, providing a clear view of the diaphragm with minimal remaining fat. The camera captures the procedure from above, showcasing the effectiveness of VATS in accessing and visualizing the right diaphragm.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid port to remove fat from the back of the sternum, while managing the patient's hypercarbia by inflating the right lung. The procedure continues in the left chest, approaching the thymoma and assessing the proximity to the pericardium before deciding to take a disc of pericardium.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the left superior vena cava (SVC) as it dives into the pericardium, joining the coronary sinus of the heart. The absence of the innominate vein is noted. The dissection proceeds on the arch of the aorta, revealing the left subclavian, left carotid, and innominate arteries, with the vagus nerve visible over the arch of the aorta.

The surgeon navigates high into the neck to access the left superior sulcus, using CO₂ insufflation to facilitate visibility. The thyro-thymic ligament and surrounding fat are exposed. A hook ligature is applied to manage sticky areas on the pericardium. The surgeon grasps the tense pericardium higher up, performing a bipolar burn to safely enter the pericardium, which is floppier lower down.

The surgeon uses a bipolar ligature to safely manipulate tissue without risk of burning the heart. The procedure progresses towards the thymoma, with the option to create a large pericardial patch from phrenic to phrenic without the need for reconstruction.

The surgeon uses a burning technique to safely remove the pericardial patch, visualizing the left phrenic nerve and left superior vena cava while approaching the arch of the aorta. The thymus is lifted from the aorta, revealing its interdigitated fronds between the head and neck vessels. Wristed instruments are used to maneuver around small attachments and clear the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid port to access the surgical field after removing the thymus from the diaphragm. The carotid artery is visible below the ligature, with the left subclavian artery just out of view at the bottom of the image. The surgeon divides the thyro-thymic ligament above the superior horn, pulling hard to expose the head and neck vessels.

The surgeon clears fatty tissue interdigitated between vessels around the arch of the aorta, safely dissecting the pericardial reflection. The pericardium is divided towards the right phrenic nerve, creating a large disc under the thymoma. A sub-xiphoid camera is used to visualize the right phrenic nerve without right-sided incisions, revealing the lateral mammary artery on the right side.

Using a sub-xiphoid ten-millimeter camera, the surgeon performs intrapericardial dissection to expose the right phrenic nerve, ensuring it is clearly visible. The surgeon uses a bipolar ligature and hook diathermy to carefully dissect along the pericardium, avoiding injury to the superior vena cava (SVC). Instruments, including a wristed Johan, are used to manipulate tissues within the chest cavity.

The surgeon identifies a large, cord-like piece of tissue near the thymoma, which appears vascular. Upon further investigation, a significant vein is discovered, likely the thymic vein, which is dissected using a hook and secured with a ligature.

The surgeon examines the arch of the aorta, identifying the left subclavian artery and carotid artery. The dissection proceeds across the mediastinum to the right superior horn, with visible manipulation of the SVC and identification of the reamer vein. A small vein, possibly a vestigial innominate vein, is divided using a ligature, allowing the right horn to be pulled medially.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera to visualize the innominate artery beneath the ligature, ensuring the safety of the surrounding structures. The right superior horn is identified lying on the innominate artery. The surgeon pulls the tissue upwards with the assisting arm to expose the area, aiming to dissect down to the trachea and remove the surrounding fat. The carotid artery and interdigitating tissue are visible, with the trachea located between the carotid and innominate arteries.

The surgeon performs dissection in the superior horn area, dividing small vessels within the thyrothymic ligament using energy devices. The trachea is visible in the background, and the final division is completed off the trachea. The right lung is inflated but does not obstruct the view. The arch of the aorta, innominate, carotid, and subclavian arteries are visible with minimal surrounding fat. A large thymic sample is extracted through the sub-xiphoid port, and a twenty French drain is placed through the sub-xiphoid hole.

The surgeon removes the thymus, revealing a clear view of the heart and pericardium. The left atrial appendage is visible, along with the innominate artery, left carotid, and subclavian arteries. The trachea is exposed with minimal surrounding fat, providing a clear view into the right chest.
